**Třinec** (Czech: [[ˈtr̝̊ɪnɛts]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Czech "Help:IPA/Czech") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Trinec.ogg "File:Trinec.ogg"); [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *Trzyniec*, [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Trzynietz*) is a city in [Frýdek-Místek District](/wiki/Fr%C3%BDdek-M%C3%ADstek_District "Frýdek-Místek District") in the [Moravian-Silesian Region](/wiki/Moravian-Silesian_Region "Moravian-Silesian Region") of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). It has about 34,000 inhabitants, making it the least populated [statutory city](/wiki/Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic) "Statutory city (Czech Republic)") in the country.

The city is an important cultural centre of the [Polish minority in Trans-Olza](/wiki/Polish_minority_in_the_Czech_Republic "Polish minority in the Czech Republic"), which makes up 12.1% of the population (as of 2021).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Třinec is notable for the [Třinec Iron and Steel Works](/wiki/T%C5%99inec_Iron_and_Steel_Works "Třinec Iron and Steel Works") steel plant, the largest in the country, which still has a major impact on the city, its character, demographics, and [air quality](/wiki/Air_quality "Air quality").

## Administrative division

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=T%C5%99inec&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name Třinec is of topographic origin, derived from the Slavic word for [reed](/wiki/Reed_(plant) "Reed (plant)") (Polish: *trzcina*, Czech: *třtina*).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=T%C5%99inec&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Třinec is located about 22 kilometres (14 mi) east of [Frýdek-Místek](/wiki/Fr%C3%BDdek-M%C3%ADstek "Frýdek-Místek") and 30 km (19 mi) southeast of [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava"). It lies in the historical region of [Cieszyn Silesia](/wiki/Cieszyn_Silesia "Cieszyn Silesia"), on the border with [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and also near the border with [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), which is about 20 km (12 mi) of the city centre.

Třinec lies on the [Olza](/wiki/Olza_(river) "Olza (river)") River. The [Tyra](/wiki/Tyra_(stream) "Tyra (stream)") Stream flows across the municipal territory and joins the Olza in the city centre. The elevation of the built-up area is approximately 300 m (980 ft) above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"). The northern part of the municipal territory is located in the [Moravian-Silesian Foothills](/wiki/Moravian-Silesian_Foothills "Moravian-Silesian Foothills"). The southern, sparsely populated part is located in the [Moravian-Silesian Beskids](/wiki/Moravian-Silesian_Beskids "Moravian-Silesian Beskids") mountain range, which also belongs to the [Beskydy Protected Landscape Area](/wiki/Beskydy_Protected_Landscape_Area "Beskydy Protected Landscape Area"). The municipal border runs along the peaks of several mountains, including Ostrý (with an elevation of 1,044 metres (3,425 ft) the highest point in Třinec), Smrčina at 1,015 m (3,330 ft), Šindelná at 1,000 m (3,300 ft), and Javorový at 1,032 m (3,386 ft).

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=T%C5%99inec&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Třinec has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Cfb* in the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")).

The first written mention of Třinec is from 1444, but the village was probably founded already in the second half of the 14th century.[[5]](#cite_note-history-5) Politically, the village then belonged to the [Duchy of Teschen](/wiki/Duchy_of_Teschen "Duchy of Teschen"), a [fee](/wiki/Fee_(feudal_tenure) "Fee (feudal tenure)") of the [Kingdom of Bohemia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia"), which after 1526 became part of the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). In 1770, the village had about 200 inhabitants and was completely agricultural.[[5]](#cite_note-history-5)

The area was rich in [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore") deposits and had sufficient water energy and a high supply of wood, which were the main reasons to establish an [iron works](/wiki/Iron_works "Iron works") there. The iron mill began operation in 1839, becoming the largest in the entire [Cieszyn Silesia](/wiki/Cieszyn_Silesia "Cieszyn Silesia"), and became a major milestone in the history of the village, which reoriented itself to industry. After the construction of the [Košice–Bohumín Railway](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice%E2%80%93Bohum%C3%ADn_Railway "Košice–Bohumín Railway") line in 1871, rapid development of the town took place.[[6]](#cite_note-about-6)

After the [revolutions of 1848 in the Austrian Empire](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848_in_the_Austrian_Empire "Revolutions of 1848 in the Austrian Empire"), a modern [municipal division](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") was introduced in the re-established [Austrian Silesia](/wiki/Austrian_Silesia "Austrian Silesia"). The village as a municipality was added to the [political](/wiki/Districts_of_Austria "Districts of Austria") and [legal district](/wiki/Legal_district "Legal district") of [Cieszyn](/wiki/Cieszyn "Cieszyn"). According to censuses conducted in 1880–1910, the population of the municipality grew from 1,792 in 1880 to 3,849 in 1910, with a majority being native Polish speakers (growing from 51.4% in 1880 to 96.6% in 1900 and 96% in 1910), accompanied by a German-speaking minority (at most 32.5% in 1880, then dropping to 12.2% in 1900, and up to 24.3% in 1910) and Czech speakers (peaking in 1890 at 17.4%, then dropping to 6.7% in 1910). In terms of religion, in 1910 the majority were [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics") (63.2%), followed by [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") (34.5%), and [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") (1.9%).[[7]](#cite_note-7) The village was also traditionally inhabited by [Cieszyn Vlachs](/wiki/Cieszyn_Vlachs "Cieszyn Vlachs"), speaking the [Cieszyn Silesian dialect](/wiki/Cieszyn_Silesian_dialect "Cieszyn Silesian dialect").

After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the fall of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), the [Polish–Czechoslovak War](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Czechoslovak_War "Polish–Czechoslovak War"), and the division of [Cieszyn Silesia](/wiki/Cieszyn_Silesia "Cieszyn Silesia") in 1920, it became a part of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Republic_(1918-1938) "Czechoslovak Republic (1918-1938)"). In 1931, Třinec was promoted to a town.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Following the [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement") in October 1938, together with the [Trans-Olza](/wiki/Trans-Olza "Trans-Olza") region, it was annexed by [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"), administratively adjoined to [Cieszyn County](/wiki/Cieszyn_County "Cieszyn County") of the [Silesian Voivodeship](/wiki/Silesian_Voivodeship_(1920%E2%80%9339) "Silesian Voivodeship (1920–39)").[[9]](#cite_note-9) It was then annexed by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") at the beginning of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). After the war, it was restored to [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia").

In 1946, the villages of [Lyžbice](/wiki/Ly%C5%BEbice "Lyžbice"), [Dolní Líštná](/wiki/Doln%C3%AD_L%C3%AD%C5%A1tn%C3%A1 "Dolní Líštná"), and [Konská](/wiki/Konsk%C3%A1_(T%C5%99inec) "Konská (Třinec)") were joined to Třinec. In 1956–1977, a large housing estate was built in Lyžbice, and it became the most populated town part of Třinec.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Afterwards, Lyžbice became a new [downtown](/wiki/Downtown "Downtown"), taking the place of [Staré Město](/wiki/Star%C3%A9_M%C4%9Bsto_(T%C5%99inec) "Staré Město (Třinec)") (lit. 'old town').

The municipalities of [Guty](/wiki/Guty_(T%C5%99inec) "Guty (Třinec)"), [Karpentná](/wiki/Karpentn%C3%A1 "Karpentná"), [Nebory](/wiki/Nebory "Nebory"), [Oldřichovice](/wiki/Old%C5%99ichovice_(T%C5%99inec) "Oldřichovice (Třinec)"), [Ropice](/wiki/Ropice "Ropice"), [Tyra](/wiki/Tyra_(T%C5%99inec) "Tyra (Třinec)"), and [Vendryně](/wiki/Vendryn%C4%9B "Vendryně") were joined to Třinec in 1980. However, Vendryně became an independent municipality in 1995 and Ropice followed in 2000.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 2018, Třinec became a [statutory city](/wiki/Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic) "Statutory city (Czech Republic)").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=T%C5%99inec&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/T%C5%99ineck%C3%A9_%C5%BEelez%C3%A1rny.jpg/250px-T%C5%99ineck%C3%A9_%C5%BEelez%C3%A1rny.jpg)](/wiki/File:T%C5%99ineck%C3%A9_%C5%BEelez%C3%A1rny.jpg)

Třinec Iron and Steel Works

The [Třinec Iron and Steel Works](/wiki/T%C5%99inec_Iron_and_Steel_Works "Třinec Iron and Steel Works") company is one of the largest employers in the Czech Republic. Including smaller production plants outside Třinec, the company employs 7,000 people (as of 2021).[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=T%C5%99inec&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/T%C5%99inec_evang._kostel_2010.JPG/250px-T%C5%99inec_evang._kostel_2010.JPG)](/wiki/File:T%C5%99inec_evang._kostel_2010.JPG)

The city's [Evangelical](/wiki/Silesian_Evangelical_Church_of_the_Augsburg_Confession "Silesian Evangelical Church of the Augsburg Confession") church

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/2023_Guty_church_replica.jpg/250px-2023_Guty_church_replica.jpg)](/wiki/File:2023_Guty_church_replica.jpg)

Replica of Church of Corpus Cristi

The year's biggest event is the *Hutnický den* ("Metallurgy Day"), which features numerous live performances from bands and artists from all over the country and abroad. It takes place every year in May, but was not held in 2020 and 2021.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

From 1993 to 2012, one of the oldest and most famous rock festivals in the Czech Republic, *Noc plná hvězd* ("star-studded night"), was held here every year.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=T%C5%99inec&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sport")]

The city is represented by the successful ice hockey team [HC Oceláři Třinec](/wiki/HC_Ocel%C3%A1%C5%99i_T%C5%99inec "HC Oceláři Třinec"), which has been playing in the [Czech Extraliga](/wiki/Czech_Extraliga "Czech Extraliga") since 1995 and has won six times. Oceláři (Steelers) play their home games at [Werk Arena](/wiki/Werk_Arena "Werk Arena"), which opened in 2014 and has a seating capacity of 5,400.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The arena also hosted the [Team Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_Davis_Cup_team "Czech Republic Davis Cup team") at the [2016 Davis Cup World Group](/wiki/2016_Davis_Cup_World_Group "2016 Davis Cup World Group").

The city's football team, [FK Třinec](/wiki/FK_T%C5%99inec "FK Třinec"), plays in the [Moravian-Silesian Football League](/wiki/Moravian-Silesian_Football_League "Moravian-Silesian Football League") (third tier of the Czech football system).

The floorball club [FBC Třinec](/wiki/FBC_T%C5%99inec "FBC Třinec") has been playing in the [first tier of the women's competition](/wiki/Extraliga_%C5%BEen_ve_florbale "Extraliga žen ve florbale") since 2023 and in the third tier of the men's competition. The team plays its home games in the [Sports Hall STARS Třinec](/wiki/Sports_Hall_STARS_T%C5%99inec "Sports Hall STARS Třinec").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Třinec also has an [athletics](/wiki/Track_and_field_athletics "Track and field athletics") club, founded in 1951.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Sports that have a tradition in Třinec include orienteering, weightlifting, Greco-Roman wrestling, road cycling, and chess.[[6]](#cite_note-about-6)

## Sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=T%C5%99inec&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sights")]

Třinec is poor in monuments. The main historic landmark is the Church of Saint Albert, built in the 1880s.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

A notable building is the wooden Church of Corpus Cristi in Guty. The original church from the 16th century was destroyed by a [deliberate fire](/wiki/Arson "Arson") in 2017. In 2021, a replica was completed on its site.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 1969, Třinec Iron and Steel Works opened a company museum, which has been jointly operated with the city as the museum of both the company and the city since 1992.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
